---
title: "Actual versus Statutory Tariff Rates: Measuring and Decomposing the Gap in the 2025--2026 U.S. Tariff Episode"
author:
  - Abhi Gupta
  - John Iselin
  - John Ricco
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %Y')`"
abstract: |
  The 2025--2026 U.S. tariff escalation drove announced statutory tariff rates
  into the mid-teens, yet Treasury collected duties at barely two-thirds of that
  level. We measure this gap directly and decompose it into interpretable
  channels. Building per-cell statutory rates from the Harmonized Tariff Schedule
  and Chapter 99 authorities and pairing them with Census import microdata, we
  construct a ladder of effective tariff rate (ETR) measures that moves stepwise
  from an announced statutory rate to the rate Treasury actually realizes. Each
  rung turns on one channel: USMCA claim-rate normalization, import composition,
  non-USMCA preference claiming, a statutory-versus-declared residual, and
  declared-versus-collected timing. As of March 2026 --- the first full month
  after the Supreme Court struck down the IEEPA tariffs --- the announced statutory
  rate had fallen to 11.45\% and the realized rate to 7.34\%, a gap of 4.10 pp;
  earlier in the episode the announced rate exceeded 16\% and the gap topped 8 pp
  (April 2025). Import composition --- overwhelmingly a fall in
  China's import share --- accounts for roughly 2--3 pp consistently, while the
  back-end residual dominates and is the least-explained channel. An independent
  cross-check reproduces the December 2025 figures of Eck et al. (2026) within
  0.6 pp on every comparable quantity.
output:
  pdf_document:
    number_sections: true
    toc: false
    keep_tex: true
    latex_engine: xelatex
fontsize: 11pt
geometry: margin=1in
bibliography: refs.bib
link-citations: true
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{longtable}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \usepackage{amsmath}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center", out.width = "85%", fig.pos = "H"
)

# --- Paths (relative to this .Rmd, which lives in paper/) ---------------------
fig_dir <- "../results/figures"
tbl_dir <- "../results/tables"

rd <- function(f) {
  p <- file.path(tbl_dir, f)
  if (file.exists(p)) utils::read.csv(p, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) else NULL
}
fig <- function(f) {
  # prefer the clean (no-title) version; captions are supplied by fig.cap
  file.path(fig_dir, f)
}

# --- Load output tables -------------------------------------------------------
decomp     <- rd("decomp_monthly.csv")          # 6-tier monthly: s0..s4,t + gaps
div_cty    <- rd("diversion_by_country_avg.csv") # period-mean composition by partner
div_prod   <- rd("diversion_by_product_avg.csv") # period-mean composition by product
cty_avg    <- rd("counterfactual_by_country_avg.csv")

# --- Convenience accessors ----------------------------------------------------
gv <- function(ym_, col) if (!is.null(decomp)) decomp[[col]][decomp$ym == ym_] else NA
f1 <- function(x) formatC(x, format = "f", digits = 1)
f2 <- function(x) formatC(x, format = "f", digits = 2)

# Headline months
ann_dec <- gv("2025m12", "s1"); real_dec <- gv("2025m12", "t")
gap_dec <- ann_dec - real_dec

# Latest available month (auto-tracks the data; e.g. "2026m3" -> "March 2026")
ym_last  <- if (!is.null(decomp)) decomp$ym[nrow(decomp)] else NA
.mk      <- suppressWarnings(as.integer(sub(".*m", "", ym_last)))
.yr      <- sub("m.*", "", ym_last)
ym_lab   <- if (!is.na(.mk)) paste(month.name[.mk], .yr) else ym_last
ann_last <- gv(ym_last, "s1"); real_last <- gv(ym_last, "t")
comp_last <- gv(ym_last, "s2"); pref_last <- gv(ym_last, "s3"); cens_last <- gv(ym_last, "s4")
gap_last <- ann_last - real_last

# Policy-window definitions (ym strings) used for the sub-period table
windows <- list(
  "Overall (25m1--26m3)"          = decomp$ym,
  "Pre-Liberation (25m1--3)"      = c("2025m1","2025m2","2025m3"),
  "Lib. to Phase 2 (25m4--6)"     = c("2025m4","2025m5","2025m6"),
  "Phase 2 (25m7)"                = c("2025m7"),
  "Recip. to SCOTUS (25m8--26m1)" = c("2025m8","2025m9","2025m10","2025m11","2025m12","2026m1"),
  "Post-SCOTUS (26m2--3)"         = c("2026m2","2026m3")
)
```

# Introduction

The 2025--2026 reorganization of U.S. trade policy was the largest in nearly a
century. A stack of overlapping authorities --- IEEPA reciprocal and fentanyl
tariffs, expanded Section 232 metals and autos duties, the legacy Section 301
China lists, and a post-litigation Section 122 blanket --- pushed the
trade-weighted *announced* statutory tariff rate from low single digits into the
mid-teens within a few months. Yet the duties the Treasury actually collected
never came close to the announced schedule. By our preferred summary measure,
the announced statutory effective tariff rate (ETR) was
`r f2(ann_last)`\% in `r ym_lab` while the realized rate --- customs duties
collected as a share of import value --- was `r f2(real_last)`\%, a gap of
`r f2(gap_last)` percentage points (pp). The gap was wider still earlier in the
episode, exceeding 8 pp in April 2025 in the weeks after "Liberation Day," and
the announced level has since fallen sharply --- from a peak above 16\% in
late 2025 to `r f2(ann_last)`\% --- as the Supreme Court's February 2026 ruling
struck down the IEEPA tariffs.

This divergence is not a measurement artifact, and it is not small. It matters
for at least three reasons.

- **Revenue forecasting.** Scoring agencies anchor tariff-revenue projections on
  assumed effective rates. A persistent, structured gap between announced and
  realized rates implies systematic over-projection unless the channels driving
  the gap are understood and modeled.
- **Inference about economic responses.** Pass-through, welfare, and elasticity
  estimates that use the statutory rate as the treatment intensity will overstate
  the tariff "bite" if the realized rate is materially smaller. @gopinath_neiman_2026
  show that pass-through on *collected* duties is near-complete, so the gap ---
  not under-implementation of the schedule --- is what shields prices.
- **Policy evaluation.** The realized burden borne by importers, consumers, and
  partner countries equals what the announced rate implies only if the gap is
  zero. Decomposing the gap tells us which margins are behavioral, which are
  administrative, and which are simply timing.

We make three contributions. First, we **measure** the gap directly and
transparently: per-cell statutory rates are reconstructed from the Harmonized
Tariff Schedule (HTS) and Chapter 99 text, day-weighted across mid-month policy
changes, and aggregated against Census import weights, so the announced rate we
report is reproducible from primary sources rather than asserted. Second, we
**decompose** the gap into a sequential ladder of ETR measures in which each rung
turns on exactly one channel --- USMCA claim-rate normalization, import
composition, non-USMCA preference claiming, and a back-end residual that we split
into a statutory-versus-declared component and a declared-versus-collected timing
component. Existing decompositions [@azzimonti_2025; @eck_etal_2026] bundle
several of these together. Third, we **attribute** every decomposable channel to
partner countries and product groups using a symmetric Shapley two-way split,
so the same aggregate gap can be read along orthogonal cuts. We validate the
framework against the independent estimates of @eck_etal_2026 and reproduce their
December 2025 numbers within 0.6 pp on every comparable quantity.

The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 reviews the literature
on the statutory--actual gap. Section 3 sets out the framework, the
mathematical decomposition, and the data. Section 4 presents the headline
comparison and the aggregate channel decomposition. Section 5 attributes the
gap by partner country and by product group. Section 6 discusses open questions
and future work.

# Literature

The divergence between announced and realized tariff rates has become one of
the defining empirical features of the 2025 episode, and a small but fast-moving
literature has formed around it.

**Documenting the gap.** @gopinath_neiman_2026 provide the most comprehensive
aggregate treatment, documenting a roughly 27\% trade-weighted statutory rate
against a realized rate near 10--11\% in September 2025 and attributing the gap
to shipping lags, exemptions, trade-agreement utilization, and enforcement.
Their central result for our purposes is that pass-through on *collected* duties
is near-complete (about 94\% in 2025, up from 80\% in 2018--2019): the gap is
what moderates prices, which makes understanding its composition the welfare
question. @azzimonti_2025 supplies a granular single-month decomposition for May
2025, splitting an 8.8 pp gap into within-country product mix (0.6 pp),
cross-country share shifts (2.7 pp), and implementation frictions (5.5 pp); her
Canada case study finds that 31\% of Canadian product lines with positive
predicted tariffs generated zero duty.

**The direct comparator.** @eck_etal_2026 offer the closest analogue to this
paper. They decompose the announced-versus-realized gap into a composition
channel and a rate-discrepancy channel using Laspeyres-style baseline weights,
and they contrast 2025 with 2018--2019. By December 2025 the announced ETR
reached 14.7\% and the realized rate fell 5.43 pp short --- about 44\% of the
announced increase, double the 22\% fraction of the far more concentrated
2018--2019 episode. They emphasize that 2025 composition shifts began
*immediately*, unlike the gradual substitution of the earlier episode, and
predict that frictional gaps compress as front-loading depletes. We adopt their
two-channel framing as a benchmark and refine it into a finer ladder.

**Tracking series.** The Penn Wharton Budget Model [@pwbm_2026] tracks realized
rates monthly and splits revenue into mechanical and behavioral components,
documenting the surge in USMCA compliance from roughly 33--38\% of Canadian and
Mexican imports in 2024 to nearly 88\% by December 2025. The Budget Lab at Yale
[@ybl_2026] maintains a complementary public series and emphasizes the
pre-versus-post-substitution distinction; this paper is its analytical companion.

**Measurement and mechanism.** @waugh_2026 argues that both the statutory
average and the duties-to-imports ratio understate the welfare burden, because
dispersion matters: building on @anderson_neary_1996, his Trade Restrictiveness
Index implies a welfare-equivalent uniform rate of roughly 21--23\% in late
2025, about twice the realized rate. On the price side, @cavallo_llamas_vazquez_2025
find about 20\% retail pass-through after six months (contributing ~0.7 pp to
CPI), while @cavallo_etal_2021 and @flaaen_etal_2025 document border and
store-level pass-through, and @cui_etal_2025 provide a firm-strategy framework
(mitigation versus contingency) that rationalizes a partially structural gap.
For historical and fiscal context, @fajgelbaum_etal_2020 analyze the 2018--2019
episode, @alfaro_chor_2025 and @barbiero_etal_2025 document supply-chain
reallocation, @clausing_obstfeld_2025 treat tariffs as fiscal policy, and
@gerlach_2025 places the current ~10--12\% realized rate in a 150-year context.

Where this paper fits: the existing work opens one or two channels at a time. We
provide a single unified ladder spanning USMCA claim-rate evolution,
cross-country and within-country composition, non-USMCA preferences, a structural
collection residual, and Treasury timing --- with a clean attribution to
countries and products at every channel except the aggregate-only timing rung.

# Methodology

## Framework and mathematical setup

### The ETR ladder

For any month $t$, the effective tariff rate is total duties over total import
value. Indexing cells by product $p$ (HTS-10) and partner country $c$, and
letting $w_{pc}(t)$ be the cell's import value and $r_{pc}(t)$ its tariff rate,

$$
\tau_t \;=\; \frac{R_t}{I_t} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{p,c} w_{pc}(t)\, r_{pc}(t)}{\sum_{p,c} w_{pc}(t)}.
$$

We construct six measures of $\tau_t$ that differ only in *which* rate panel and
*which* weight panel enter the average. We give each a descriptive name and use
those names throughout; Table \ref{tab:tiers} is the master key.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{The ETR ladder. All rate panels share the same authority stacking, metal-content rules, and MFN-exemption logic; they differ only in the USMCA claim-rate input. The two observed measures (Census-declared, Treasury-realized) are data, not statutory reconstructions.}
\label{tab:tiers}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
Measure & Rate input & Weight input \\
\midrule
Baseline statutory      & statutory, USMCA at 2024 claim rates        & 2024 imports \\
Announced statutory      & statutory, USMCA at post-July 2025 rates    & 2024 imports \\
Composition-adjusted     & statutory, USMCA at post-July 2025 rates    & monthly imports \\
Preference-adjusted      & + non-USMCA preferences applied             & monthly imports \\
Census-declared          & Census calculated duty / import value       & (observed) \\
Treasury-realized        & Treasury customs duties / goods imports     & (observed) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The **announced statutory rate** is the framework anchor. It applies the
post-July 2025 USMCA claim rates (~89\% for Canada and Mexico) to fixed 2024
import weights, and by construction it equals the public Budget Lab tracker's
daily statutory ETR collapsed to monthly. Stepping down the ladder turns on one
channel at a time:

- **USMCA adjustment** (baseline $\to$ announced): hold 2024 weights and move the
  USMCA claim rate from its 2024 level (~38\% Canada, ~50\% Mexico) to the
  post-July 2025 level. This is largely retrospective --- firms filed claims late
  and a July 2025 USITC reporting change made utilization visible --- so we treat
  it as baseline backstory rather than a behavioral channel.
- **Import composition** (announced $\to$ composition-adjusted): hold rates fixed
  and shift weights from 2024 to actual monthly imports. This isolates the effect
  of the changing import basket. We call it *import composition* rather than
  "trade diversion" because the construction reweights cells but does not separate
  anticipatory front-loading from durable supplier diversion.
- **Non-USMCA preferences** (composition-adjusted $\to$ preference-adjusted):
  apply observed claim shares for Annex II / ITA / Chapter 98, KORUS, GSP/AGOA,
  and other bilateral FTAs.
- **Residual** (preference-adjusted $\to$ Census-declared) and **timing**
  (Census-declared $\to$ Treasury-realized): the back-end gap between the
  fully-adjusted statutory reconstruction and what is actually collected. Treasury
  duties are reported only in aggregate, so the policy-relevant object is the
  combined preference-adjusted-to-realized residual; the Census-declared measure
  is an observable *approximation* that lets us look inside it, not a fourth
  statutory step.

These channels are additive by construction: the announced-to-realized gap is
the sum of import composition, non-USMCA preferences, residual, and timing, and
the full gap adds the USMCA-adjustment backstory.

### Decomposing import composition: between and within groups

The import-composition channel holds rates fixed and moves weights, which makes
it amenable to a clean group decomposition. Partition cells into groups $g$
(eight partner groups, or nine product groups). The aggregate rate is the
share-weighted average of group rates,

$$
\tau \;=\; \sum_g s_g R_g, \qquad
R_g = \frac{\sum_{i \in g} w_i r_i}{\sum_{i \in g} w_i}, \qquad
s_g = \frac{\sum_{i \in g} w_i}{\sum_i w_i}.
$$

Comparing two weight panels (2024 weights, superscript $0$; monthly weights,
superscript $1$) at the *same* rates, the symmetric Shapley two-way split
averages the two orderings of "share change then rate change":

$$
\Delta\tau \;=\;
\underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_g \bigl(R^0_g + R^1_g\bigr)\bigl(s^1_g - s^0_g\bigr)}_{\text{between-group: cross-group share shifts}}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_g \bigl(s^0_g + s^1_g\bigr)\bigl(R^1_g - R^0_g\bigr)}_{\text{within-group: mix shifts inside each group}}.
$$

A positive group contribution adds to the import-composition gap. Because the
rate panel is fixed, the *between* term captures a group gaining or losing share
of total imports, and the *within* term captures the group's internal product
mix tilting toward lower- or higher-tariff cells. The country and product
partitions are complementary: both sum to the same aggregate gap each month, a
restriction we assert at runtime to within numerical noise.

### Per-group attribution for the fixed-weight channels

For channels where weights are held fixed and rates change (non-USMCA
preferences; the statutory-versus-declared residual), the between-group Shapley
term is mechanically zero and the attribution collapses to a dollar-weighted rate
difference,

$$
\text{contribution}_g \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i \in g}\bigl(r^{\text{left}}_i - r^{\text{right}}_i\bigr)\, w_i}{\sum_i w_i},
$$

where the right-hand rate is the next rung down (for the residual, the
cell-level Census-declared rate). The timing channel is reported in aggregate
only, since Treasury revenue carries no partner or product breakout.

### Per-cell rate construction and preference mechanics

Cell-level statutory rates are built by the Budget Lab tariff-rate tracker from
HTSUS and Chapter 99 text. Each cell stacks authority components --- MFN base,
IEEPA reciprocal, IEEPA fentanyl, Section 232, Section 301, Section 122 --- with
metal-content scaling for Section 232 (the metal share carries the 232 rate; the
non-metal share carries reciprocal and 122), country-specific stacking rules,
USMCA scaling by claim share (with a 0.40 U.S.-content rule for auto and
medium/heavy-duty parts), HS2-by-country MFN exemption shares, and IEEPA floor
adjustments. All six measures use the *same* stack; only the USMCA claim-rate
input differs across the four statutory panels.

The non-USMCA preference step subtracts, per cell, the authority components that
each preference exempts. Letting $s_q$ be the observed claim share for preference
$q$ (from importer-declared codes in the Census microdata), the
preference-adjusted rate is the composition-adjusted rate minus

$$
\Delta^{\text{base}} = \bigl(s_{\text{duty\_free}} + s_{\text{korus}} + s_{\text{gsp}} + s_{\text{other\_fta}}\bigr)\, r^{\text{base}},
\qquad
\Delta^{\text{recip}} = s_{\text{duty\_free}}^{\text{annex}}\, r^{\text{recip}},
$$

floored at zero. The duty-free channel reduces the MFN base for all of its
imports, but reduces the IEEPA reciprocal layer only for the share whose HS10 is
on the controlling Annex II / ITA / Chapter 98 exempt list
($s_{\text{duty\_free}}^{\text{annex}}$); pure MFN-zero imports that are not on
that list still owe the reciprocal duty statutorily, so they are not credited
with a reciprocal exemption. KORUS, GSP/AGOA, and other FTAs reduce the MFN base
only. Because preference claims are mutually exclusive at the entry level, the
shares partition imports and the rung is structurally non-negative.

## Data

Four sources populate the framework; Table \ref{tab:data} maps each to the
formal model.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Data sources and their role in the framework.}
\label{tab:data}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
Source & Granularity & Role \\
\midrule
Census IMDB              & HS10 $\times$ country $\times$ month & monthly weights $w_{pc}(t)$; Census-declared duties \\
Tariff Rate Tracker      & HS10 $\times$ country $\times$ revision & cell-level statutory rates $r_{pc}(t)$ \\
USITC DataWeb            & HS10 $\times$ country $\times$ month & USMCA claim-rate scenarios \\
U.S. Treasury (MTS/DTS)  & aggregate, monthly & realized rate (cash duties / imports) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The analysis window is January 2025 through March 2026, the latest month with
Census import microdata available at the time of writing. Three measurement
choices are worth stating explicitly. First, aggregation is a single-stage,
row-level value-weighted average over (HS10 $\times$ country) cells --- there is
no HS2 bridging. Second, zero-tariff products must remain in the denominator:
dropping them inflates the realized rate from roughly 3.4\% to roughly 27\%.
Third, every statutory rate panel is day-weighted across HTS revisions *within*
each month, so mid-month policy events (Liberation Day on April 2, 2025; Phase 2
on August 7, 2025; the SCOTUS ruling and Section 122 onset on February 24, 2026)
are captured rather than snapped to a revision boundary. The Census-declared
measure reflects duties as importers report them at entry; the Treasury-realized
measure reflects cash actually collected, so the two diverge through refunds,
drawback, FTZ deferrals, administrative-review liquidations, and payment timing.

# Basic results

## The headline gap

Figure \ref{fig:baseline} plots the two measures that matter most to an outside
analyst: the announced statutory rate and the rate Treasury realizes. Both rise
sharply through spring 2025, but a persistent wedge opens between them.

```{r baseline, fig.cap="Announced statutory rate versus Treasury-realized rate, monthly. The announced rate applies post-July 2025 USMCA claim rates to 2024 import weights; the realized rate is Treasury customs duties over goods imports."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_baseline.png"))
```

The announced statutory rate jumps from `r f2(gv("2025m3","s1"))`\% in March
2025 to `r f2(gv("2025m4","s1"))`\% in April after Liberation Day, peaks at
`r f2(gv("2025m9","s1"))`\% in September 2025, then falls steeply after the
February 2026 Supreme Court ruling invalidated the IEEPA tariffs and the regime
partially transitioned to Section 122, reaching `r f2(ann_last)`\% in `r ym_lab`.
The realized rate traces the same path one step behind:
`r f2(gv("2025m3","t"))`\% in March 2025, `r f2(gv("2025m4","t"))`\% in April,
peaking near `r f2(gv("2025m11","t"))`\% in late 2025, and falling to
`r f2(real_last)`\% in `r ym_lab`. The monthly gap ranges from about 3.4 pp to
8.8 pp; it was `r f2(gap_dec)` pp in December 2025 and `r f2(gap_last)` pp in
`r ym_lab`.

## The full ladder

Figure \ref{fig:ladder} shows all six measures together. The three statutory
reconstructions (announced, composition-adjusted, preference-adjusted) bunch
tightly after mid-2025, which means the dominant gap is between the announced
rate and what is realized --- and most of the action lives in the back-end
residual rather than in composition or preferences.

```{r ladder, fig.cap="The full ETR ladder, January 2025--March 2026. From top: baseline statutory (backstory), announced statutory (anchor), composition-adjusted, preference-adjusted, and Treasury-realized. The Census-declared measure sits between preference-adjusted and Treasury-realized."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_ladder.png"))
```

Table \ref{tab:windows} averages the ladder and the implied channels over five
policy windows. Two features stand out beyond the trend. The
statutory-versus-declared residual stays positive in every window: the cell-level
reconstruction persistently over-predicts what importers declare at entry. And
the timing channel flips sign mid-2025 --- positive early, then increasingly
negative, reaching about $-2$ pp in February 2026 (easing to about $-0.5$ pp in
March) as Treasury cash receipts come to *exceed* Census-declared duties.

```{r windows, results='asis'}
if (!is.null(decomp)) {
  cols <- c("s0","s1","s2","s3","s4","t",
            "gap_adjustment","gap_diversion","gap_others","gap_residual","gap_timing")
  wtab <- t(sapply(windows, function(yms) {
    sub <- decomp[decomp$ym %in% yms, , drop = FALSE]
    colMeans(sub[cols], na.rm = TRUE)
  }))
  wdf <- data.frame(Window = rownames(wtab), round(wtab, 2), row.names = NULL,
                    check.names = FALSE)
  colnames(wdf) <- c("Window", "Base", "Ann", "Comp", "Pref", "Cens", "Treas",
                     "USMCA", "ImpComp", "NonUSMCA", "Resid", "Timing")
  kbl <- knitr::kable(
    wdf, format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "", row.names = FALSE,
    caption = "Sub-period averages by policy window. Left block: ladder measures (\\%). Right block: channel gaps (pp). Post-SCOTUS covers only two months and should be read as directional."
  )
  if (requireNamespace("kableExtra", quietly = TRUE)) {
    kbl <- kableExtra::add_header_above(
      kbl, c(" " = 1, "Ladder measures (\\\\%)" = 6, "Channel gaps (pp)" = 5), escape = FALSE)
    kbl <- kableExtra::kable_styling(kbl, latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position"))
  }
  kbl
}
```

## The aggregate channel decomposition

Figure \ref{fig:channel} splits the announced-to-realized gap into its three
forward channels each month: import composition, non-USMCA preferences, and the
combined residual-plus-timing band. Import composition contributes a steady
2--3 pp; non-USMCA preferences are consistently the smallest band (~0.4 pp, and
larger only when IEEPA reciprocal rates and Annex II claiming were both high in
mid-2025); and
the residual-plus-timing band is the largest and most variable, expanding around
major policy events, briefly collapsing toward zero (even slightly negative) in
February 2026, then widening again to about 1.6 pp in March as the post-SCOTUS
schedule settled.

```{r channel, fig.cap="Announced-to-realized gap decomposed monthly into import composition, non-USMCA preferences, and residual-plus-timing."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_channel_stacked.png"))
```

## Validation against external estimates

Our reconstruction is independent of @eck_etal_2026 in both data assembly and
decomposition mechanics (a Shapley two-way rather than a Laspeyres separation),
yet the two converge. At December 2025 our announced rate is `r f2(ann_dec)`\%
against their 14.7\% (+0.4 pp), our realized rate is `r f2(real_dec)`\% against
their ~9.27\% implied (+0.6 pp), and our total gap is `r f2(gap_dec)` pp against
their 5.43 pp ($-0.2$ pp). Spot checks against other tracking series are
consistent: PWBM reports 10.3\% for January 2026 against our
`r f2(gv("2026m1","t"))`\%, and the Budget Lab's August 2025 range brackets our
`r f2(gv("2025m8","t"))`\% realized and `r f2(gv("2025m8","s1"))`\% announced.
The full month-by-month panel is reported in the appendix
(Table \ref{tab:xval}). Convergence of independent methodologies is a useful
check that the anchor is not idiosyncratic.

# Decompositions

We now attribute the two largest decomposable channels --- import composition and
the back-end residual --- to partner countries and product groups. The USMCA
adjustment is, by construction, almost entirely Canada and Mexico (the only
USMCA-eligible partners) and the non-USMCA preferences channel is small and
broadly distributed, so we focus the discussion here.

## By country

Figure \ref{fig:divcty} stacks each partner group's monthly contribution to the
import-composition gap. The picture is dominated by a single country.

```{r divcty, fig.cap="Import-composition channel: monthly contributions by partner group (signed; positive adds to the gap)."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_diversion_country.png"))
```

```{r divcty-nums}
chn <- if (!is.null(div_cty)) div_cty[div_cty$partner_group == "China", ] else NULL
row <- if (!is.null(div_cty)) div_cty[div_cty$partner_group == "ROW", ] else NULL
pos_between <- if (!is.null(div_cty)) sum(div_cty$c_between[div_cty$c_between > 0]) else NA
china_share <- if (!is.null(chn)) 100 * chn$c_between / pos_between else NA
```

Period-mean contributions confirm what the figure suggests. China contributes
about **`r f2(chn$c_total)` pp** to the import-composition gap, almost entirely
through the *between* term (`r f2(chn$c_between)` pp) --- its share of total U.S.
imports fell from roughly 13\% to 7\% as the reciprocal and Section 301 stack
took hold. China alone accounts for about `r f1(china_share)`\% of all positive
between-country contribution. The rest of the world shows a *negative*
contribution (`r f2(row$c_total)` pp): lower-tariff partners such as Vietnam and
India gained share, which pulls the monthly-weighted rate down. Canada and Mexico
contribute little, because their trade concentrates in high-tariff categories
whose share moved relatively little; what movement there is comes through the
*within* term (internal mix shifts), not share loss. The headline
import-composition story is, in short, overwhelmingly a China-share-decline story
mirrored by reallocation toward low-tariff origins.

## By product

The same channel partitioned by product group (Figure \ref{fig:divprod}) reveals
two distinct patterns operating at once.

```{r divprod, fig.cap="Import-composition channel: monthly contributions by product group (signed)."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_diversion_product.png"))
```

```{r divprod-tab, results='asis'}
if (!is.null(div_prod)) {
  pt <- div_prod[, c("product_group","p_between","p_within","p_total")]
  pt <- pt[order(-pt$p_total), ]
  pt[2:4] <- round(pt[2:4], 2)
  colnames(pt) <- c("Product group", "Between", "Within", "Total")
  knitr::kable(pt, format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "", row.names = FALSE,
    caption = "Period-mean contributions to the import-composition gap by product group (pp).\\label{tab:divprod}")
}
```

High-tariff goods lose *share*: Autos & Auto Parts (+0.59 pp) and Steel &
Aluminum (+0.33 pp) compressed under Section 232, and their contributions come
through the between term. Medium-tariff catch-all categories tell the opposite
story: Other Manufactured (+0.49 pp) and Electronics & Machinery (+0.34 pp)
*gained* share but tilted their internal mix toward lower-tariff sub-products, so
their contributions are dominated by the within term (and their between terms are
negative). Both Shapley terms therefore matter: the gap has a supply-side leg
(high-tariff imports compressed) and a demand-side leg (substitution toward
lower-tariff variants inside broad categories).

## Where the residual concentrates

Figure \ref{fig:heat} maps the period-averaged statutory-versus-declared gap
across the full product-by-partner grid. The gap is concentrated, not pervasive:
most cells are small. Steel & Aluminum is the loudest row across nearly every
partner --- consistent with specific-duty ad-valorem-equivalent failures and
classification effects --- with additional mass in China autos and apparel and in
Canada/Mexico pharmaceuticals.

```{r heat, fig.cap="Period-averaged statutory-versus-declared gap (pp) on the product (rows) by partner (columns) grid."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_s2s4_heatmap.png"))
```

## All channels at once

Figure \ref{fig:attr} places the four decomposable channels side by side on a
common scale, partitioned by country. The reading is clean: the USMCA adjustment
is Canada and Mexico by construction; import composition is China; non-USMCA
preferences are small and broad; and the residual shows the widest dispersion,
with Canada and Mexico prominent in mid-2025 and the EU and rest-of-world
prominent later. The aggregate-only timing channel is not shown.

```{r attr, fig.cap="The four decomposable channels by partner group on a common vertical scale. Timing (Census-declared to Treasury-realized) is aggregate-only and omitted."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_attribution_country.png"))
```

# Future work

The framework leaves several questions open, and points to concrete extensions.

**The timing sign-flip.** The clearest unexplained signal in the panel is the
back-end timing channel turning negative since mid-2025: Treasury now collects
more than Census-declared duties imply, and cumulatively (February 2025--March
2026) Treasury has over-collected by roughly \$12.1B relative to the Census
microdata (Figure \ref{fig:cumgap}). Candidate mechanisms --- catch-up payments and refund
reversals, AD/CVD administrative-review liquidations on a 12--18 month lag, FTZ
entry reclassification, and coverage differences between the Monthly Treasury
Statement and the Census import data --- push in the same direction and cannot be
separated without Treasury microdata. A phase-correction on Treasury cash
receipts would help discriminate among them.

```{r cumgap, fig.cap="Cumulative Census-declared versus Treasury-realized customs duties (\\$), cumulated from February 2025."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("figure_cumulative_duty_gap.png"))
```

**What sustains the structural residual.** The statutory-versus-declared residual
does not converge: it holds at roughly 1.4--2.6 pp regardless of policy regime.
Specific-duty AVE failures, classification engineering, and unverified preference
claiming all push the same way, and separating them likewise requires entry-level
microdata. Our companion tracker-diagnostic outputs bound how much of the residual
is measurement error versus genuine behavior and are a natural starting point.

**Separating front-loading from durable diversion.** We deliberately label the
announced-to-composition channel *import composition* rather than trade diversion,
because the construction reweights cells without identifying mechanism. Pairing
the monthly composition shifts with inventory and shipment-timing data --- or
re-running the framework on a longer horizon as front-loaded inventory depletes
--- would let us decompose composition into anticipatory and durable parts.

**Finer preference attribution.** The non-USMCA preferences channel is currently
reported in aggregate. Its largest piece is the Annex II / ITA / Chapter 98
carve-out, which uniquely exempts the IEEPA reciprocal layer; decomposing it by
HS2 chapter inside electronics, pharmaceuticals, and energy would attribute the
carve-out story across product classes. Extending the country/product Shapley
machinery, which currently runs only for import composition, to the other rungs
is mechanical given the existing helpers.

**Historical comparison and welfare.** Re-running the framework on 2017--2019
data would test whether the channel mix has structurally changed or whether
broader concentration alone explains the doubling of the gap fraction relative to
2018--2019 [@eck_etal_2026]. And because the framework already produces per-cell
rates, pairing them with the Trade Restrictiveness Index of @waugh_2026 would let
us move from a duties-to-imports accounting of the gap to a welfare-equivalent
one.

\appendix

# Cross-validation panel {-}

```{r xval, results='asis'}
if (!is.null(decomp)) {
  xv <- decomp[, c("ym","s1","t")]
  xv$gap <- xv$s1 - xv$t
  xv[2:4] <- round(xv[2:4], 2)
  colnames(xv) <- c("Month", "Announced statutory (\\%)", "Treasury-realized (\\%)", "Gap (pp)")
  knitr::kable(xv, format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, linesep = "", row.names = FALSE,
    caption = "Announced statutory versus Treasury-realized rate, full monthly panel.")
}
```

# Product taxonomy {-}

The nine product groups are policy-salient HS2 aggregations: Steel & Aluminum
(72, 73, 76; Section 232), Autos & Auto Parts (87 and derivatives; Section 232
autos), Electronics & Machinery (84, 85; Annex II / ITA and China 301),
Pharmaceuticals (30; generic exemption), Energy & Minerals (25--27; fentanyl
carve-outs), Chemicals & Plastics (28, 29, 31--40), Apparel & Textiles (50--67;
high MFN baseline), Food & Agriculture (01--24; specific duties), and Other
Manufactured (41--49, 68--71, 86, 88--97).

# References {-}
